title: Subject-matter and objectives
obligation_text: This Regulation lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data. The free movement of personal data within the Union shall be neither restricted nor prohibited for reasons connected with the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data.
business_description: This article establishes the fundamental principles of the GDPR, focusing on protecting individuals' personal data and ensuring its free movement within the EU. For controllers and processors, this means they must handle personal data responsibly, ensuring its protection while not imposing unnecessary restrictions on its flow across EU borders. This is triggered whenever personal data is processed or transferred within the EU. Organizations must balance data protection with the need for data to move freely, avoiding unnecessary barriers. A common misconception is that data protection always limits data movement, but the GDPR aims to facilitate both.

title: Subject-matter and objectives — paragraph 1
obligation_text: This Regulation lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data.
business_description: This regulation establishes the framework for how organizations must handle personal data of individuals, ensuring their privacy and data protection rights are respected. It applies whenever personal data is processed, meaning any operation performed on data, such as collection, storage, or sharing. Organizations must ensure that data processing is lawful, transparent, and secure, facilitating the free movement of data within the EU. A common misconception is that it only applies to digital data, but it covers all personal data, regardless of format.

scenario: sharing employee data with third parties
storing user data in cloud services

title: Subject-matter and objectives — paragraph 3
obligation_text: The free movement of personal data within the Union shall be neither restricted nor prohibited for reasons connected with the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data.
business_description: This provision ensures that organizations within the EU cannot restrict or prohibit the movement of personal data across member states due to concerns about data protection. It is triggered when personal data is being transferred between EU countries. Organizations must facilitate the free flow of data while still complying with data protection laws. A common misconception is that data protection requirements can justify blocking data transfers within the EU, but this is not the case. The regulation aims to balance data protection with the seamless movement of data across borders.

scenario: transferring employee data between EU offices
sharing customer data with EU partners
centralizing EU customer data in one location

title: Material scope
obligation_text: This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means and to the processing other than by automated means of personal data which form part or are intended to form part of a filing system. This Regulation does not apply to purely personal or household activity.
business_description: This regulation applies when an organization processes personal data using automated systems, like computers, or manually if the data is part of a structured filing system. It means that if your organization collects, stores, or uses personal data in these ways, you must comply with GDPR requirements. However, if the data processing is purely for personal or household activities, such as managing a personal address book, GDPR does not apply. A common misconception is that GDPR only applies to digital data, but it also covers manual records if they are part of a filing system.

title: Material scope — paragraph 1
obligation_text: This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means and to the processing other than by automated means of personal data which form part or are intended to form part of a filing system. This Regulation does not apply to purely personal or household activity.
business_description: This regulation applies when an organization processes personal data using automated systems like computers or manually if the data is part of a structured filing system. It requires organizations to comply with GDPR when handling personal data in these ways. However, it does not apply to activities that are purely personal or related to household management. A common misconception is that GDPR applies to all personal data processing, but it only covers those that are systematic or automated. Organizations must assess their data processing activities to determine if they fall under GDPR's scope.

title: Territorial scope
obligation_text: This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data in the context of the activities of an establishment of a controller or a processor in the Union, regardless of whether the processing takes place in the Union or not. It also applies to controllers or processors not established in the Union where they offer goods or services to data subjects in the Union or monitor their behaviour.
business_description: Article 3 of the GDPR clarifies when the regulation applies based on location. It applies to any organization processing personal data if they have an establishment in the EU, regardless of where the data processing occurs. It also applies to organizations outside the EU if they offer goods or services to people in the EU or monitor their behavior. This means businesses must comply with GDPR if they target EU residents, even if they are based elsewhere. A common misconception is that GDPR only applies if the data processing physically occurs within the EU, which is not the case.

scenario: US company selling to EU customers
EU branch of a global company

title: Territorial scope — paragraph 1
obligation_text: This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data in the context of the activities of an establishment of a controller or a processor in the Union, regardless of whether the processing takes place in the Union or not. It also applies to controllers or processors not established in the Union where they offer goods or services to data subjects in the Union or monitor their behaviour.
business_description: This regulation applies to any organization processing personal data if they have an establishment in the EU, even if the data processing occurs outside the EU. It also applies to organizations outside the EU if they target EU residents by offering goods or services or by monitoring their behavior. Organizations must assess whether their activities fall under these criteria to determine if they need to comply with GDPR. A common misconception is that GDPR only applies to businesses physically located in the EU, but it can also apply to non-EU businesses targeting EU residents.

scenario: US company offering services to EU customers

title: Definitions
obligation_text: For the purposes of this Regulation: 'personal data' means any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person; 'processing' means any operation performed on personal data; 'controller' means the natural or legal person which determines the purposes and means of the processing; 'processor' means a natural or legal person which processes personal data on behalf of the controller; 'personal data breach' means a breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data.
business_description: This article defines key terms used in the GDPR, which are crucial for understanding compliance obligations. For controllers and processors, it clarifies who is responsible for deciding how and why personal data is processed (the controller) and who processes data on their behalf (the processor). It also defines what constitutes personal data and processing activities. A personal data breach is any security incident leading to unauthorized access or loss of personal data. Organizations must identify their role as either controller or processor and ensure they handle personal data securely to prevent breaches. Misunderstandings often arise around the roles and responsibilities of controllers and processors.

title: Definitions — paragraph 1
obligation_text: For the purposes of this Regulation: 'personal data' means any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person; 'processing' means any operation performed on personal data; 'controller' means the natural or legal person which determines the purposes and means of the processing; 'processor' means a natural or legal person which processes personal data on behalf of the controller; 'personal data breach' means a breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data.
business_description: Personal data means any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. The scope is deliberately broad - name, email, phone number, IP address, location data, cookie identifiers, and even combinations of information that together identify someone all qualify. The key test is identifiability: if the data can be linked back to a living individual, directly or indirectly, it is personal data. Information about companies or deceased persons is not personal data. A common misconception is that only obviously identifying data like names applies - pseudonymised data where the key is held separately still counts as personal data under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

exact: any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person

business_description: This provision requires that personal data processing is lawful only if the data subject has given clear consent for their data to be used for specific purposes. Organizations must ensure they obtain explicit permission from individuals before processing their personal data. This is triggered when an organization intends to use personal data and must be able to demonstrate that consent was freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. A common misconception is that consent can be implied or bundled with other terms, but it must be a separate, clear action by the data subject.

business_description: This provision allows organizations to process personal data when it is necessary to fulfill a contract with the data subject. It is triggered when a data subject is party to a contract, and the processing is essential to deliver the contracted services or goods. Organizations must ensure that the data processing is directly related to the contract's execution. A common misconception is that any processing related to a contract is permissible, but it must be strictly necessary for the contract's performance. This basis cannot be used for processing that is merely convenient or beneficial but not essential to the contract.

It is triggered when a law or regulation requires the organization to process certain data. For example, financial institutions may need to process data to comply with anti-money laundering laws. Organizations must ensure that the processing is strictly necessary for compliance and not for other purposes. A common misconception is that any legal requirement justifies processing, but it must be a specific obligation applicable to the controller.

business_description: This provision allows for the lawful processing of personal data when it is necessary to protect someone's vital interests, such as life or physical safety. It is typically triggered in emergency situations where obtaining consent is impractical or impossible, like during medical emergencies or natural disasters. Organizations must ensure that the processing is genuinely necessary for protecting vital interests and not used as a blanket justification for other purposes. A common misconception is that this basis can be used for any important interest, but it is strictly limited to vital interests, meaning those essential for survival or safety.

Organizations must ensure that the processing is genuinely necessary for the task and not just convenient. A common misconception is that any activity with a public benefit qualifies, but the task must be clearly defined and legally recognized as serving the public interest.

business_description: Article 6.1(f) of the GDPR allows data processing when it is necessary for the legitimate interests of the data controller or a third party, provided these interests are not overridden by the data subject's rights and freedoms. This means organizations can process data without explicit consent if they have a valid reason that benefits them or a third party. However, they must carefully assess and document that their interests are not outweighed by the potential impact on individuals. A common misconception is that any business interest qualifies, but it must be legitimate and balanced against the data subject's rights.

title: Conditions for consent
obligation_text: Where processing is based on consent, the controller shall be able to demonstrate that the data subject has consented to processing of his or her personal data. If the data subject's consent is given in the context of a written declaration which also concerns other matters, the request for consent shall be presented in a manner which is clearly distinguishable from the other matters, in an intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language. The data subject shall have the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time. The withdrawal of consent shall not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal. It shall be as easy to withdraw consent as to give it.

title: Conditions applicable to child's consent in relation to information society services
obligation_text: Where point (a) of Article 6(1) applies, in relation to the offer of information society services directly to a child, the processing of the personal data of a child shall be lawful where the child is at least 16 years old. Where the child is below the age of 16 years, such processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that consent is given or authorised by the holder of parental responsibility over the child. The controller shall make reasonable efforts to verify in such cases that consent is given or authorised by the holder of parental responsibility over the child, taking into consideration available technology.
business_description: This article applies when an organization offers online services directly to children and processes their personal data. If the child is 16 or older, they can consent to data processing themselves. For children under 16, consent must be obtained from a parent or guardian. Organizations must make reasonable efforts to verify that parental consent is genuine, considering the technology available. A common misconception is that any form of consent is sufficient, but the law requires specific verification of parental consent for those under 16.

title: Processing which does not require identification
obligation_text: If the purposes for which a controller processes personal data do not or do no longer require the identification of a data subject by the controller, the controller shall not be obliged to maintain, acquire or process additional information in order to identify the data subject for the sole purpose of complying with this Regulation. Where the controller is able to demonstrate that it is not in a position to identify the data subject, the controller shall inform the data subject accordingly, if possible. In such cases, Articles 15 to 20 shall not apply except where the data subject provides additional information enabling identification.
business_description: This article applies when a data controller processes personal data without needing to identify the individual data subjects. If the controller cannot identify the data subject, they should inform the data subject if possible. This means that certain rights, like access or rectification, do not apply unless the data subject provides information that allows them to be identified. A common misconception is that controllers must always be able to identify data subjects, which is not the case here.

This is relevant in situations where data is anonymized or pseudonymized to the extent that identification is not possible. title: Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject
obligation_text: The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language, in particular for any information addressed specifically to a child. The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request. Where the controller has reasonable doubts concerning the identity of the natural person making the request, the controller may request the provision of additional information necessary to confirm the identity of the data subject.

business_description: The right to rectification allows individuals to request corrections to their personal data if it is inaccurate or incomplete. This obligation is triggered when a data subject identifies errors or omissions in their data held by an organization. Controllers must act promptly to rectify the data, ensuring it accurately reflects the individual's information. Organizations should have processes in place to handle such requests efficiently. A common misconception is that rectification only applies to factual errors, but it also includes completing incomplete data. Controllers are not required to verify the accuracy of data unless a rectification request is made.

A common misconception is that organizations can delay rectification until it is convenient, but the requirement is to act without undue delay. This means promptly updating records to ensure data accuracy and completeness, considering the purpose for which the data is processed.

business_description: The right to erasure, also known as the 'right to be forgotten', allows individuals to request the deletion of their personal data by an organization. This right is triggered when the data is no longer necessary for its original purpose or when the individual withdraws consent and no other legal basis for processing exists. Organizations must act promptly to erase the data upon receiving a valid request. A common misconception is that this right is absolute; however, it only applies under specific conditions outlined in the regulation. Organizations should have processes in place to assess and respond to such requests efficiently.

This is triggered when the data is no longer necessary, consent is withdrawn without another legal basis, the data subject objects and there are no overriding reasons to retain the data, the data was unlawfully processed, or erasure is needed to comply with legal obligations. Organizations must have processes to assess and act on these requests promptly. A common misconception is that this right is absolute, but it is subject to specific conditions and exceptions.

Organizations should have procedures to evaluate and respond to such requests promptly.

business_description: This provision requires organizations to delete personal data when it is necessary to comply with a legal obligation. This means that if a law or regulation mandates the erasure of certain data, the organization must act accordingly. This obligation is triggered when a specific legal requirement dictates that data cannot be retained. Organizations should be aware of relevant laws that might necessitate data deletion, such as tax regulations or court orders. A common misconception is that this requirement applies only when a data subject requests erasure, but it can also be triggered independently by legal obligations.

title: Right to restriction of processing
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure;

ISO27001:2022:A.5.15.rationale: Access control can implement processing restrictions technically — flagging restricted records and preventing further processing.

business_description: The right to restriction of processing allows individuals to request that an organization temporarily limit the use of their personal data. This right is triggered when there is a dispute about the accuracy of the data or if the data processing is unlawful and the individual prefers restriction over deletion. Organizations must pause processing activities, ensuring data is not altered or used beyond storage. A common misconception is that restriction means deletion, but it actually requires maintaining the data in its current state while the issue is resolved.

title: Right to restriction of processing — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data but they are required by the data subject for legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Art.21(1) pending verification.
business_description: The right to restriction of processing allows individuals to request that an organization temporarily limit the use of their personal data under certain conditions. This right is triggered when the accuracy of the data is disputed, the processing is unlawful but the individual prefers restriction over deletion, the data is no longer needed by the organization but is required by the individual for legal reasons, or when an objection to processing is made and is under review. Organizations must be prepared to pause processing activities in these cases, ensuring that data is not used or altered until the issue is resolved.

title: Right to restriction of processing — 1(a)
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested;
business_description: This provision requires organizations to temporarily halt the processing of personal data when its accuracy is disputed by the data subject. When a data subject challenges the correctness of their data, the organization must pause processing activities related to that data until its accuracy is verified. This ensures that incorrect or misleading data is not used in decision-making processes. Organizations should have procedures to handle such requests efficiently and communicate with the data subject about the status of their request. A common misconception is that restriction means deletion, but it only involves pausing processing, not removing the data.

title: Right to restriction of processing — 1(b)
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure;
business_description: This provision requires organizations to limit the processing of personal data when the data subject believes the processing is unlawful but prefers restriction over deletion. This situation arises when individuals want their data preserved for potential legal claims or other reasons, despite the processing being unlawful. Organizations must halt most processing activities, ensuring data is only stored and not used for other purposes until the issue is resolved. A common misconception is that restriction means deletion, but it actually involves maintaining the data in a way that prevents further processing while keeping it intact.

title: Right to restriction of processing — 1(c)
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data but they are required by the data subject for legal claims;
business_description: This provision of the GDPR allows individuals to request that their personal data be restricted from processing when the organization no longer needs it, but the individual requires it for legal purposes. In practice, this means that if a data subject informs you they need their data preserved for a legal claim, you must halt any further processing of that data, except for storage, until the issue is resolved. Organizations often misunderstand this as a requirement to delete data, but it actually means to pause processing while retaining the data for the individual's legal needs.

title: Right to restriction of processing — 1(d)
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Art.21(1) pending verification.
business_description: When a data subject objects to the processing of their personal data under Article 21(1) of the GDPR, the organization must temporarily restrict processing until the objection is verified. This means that the data cannot be actively processed, but it can be stored. Organizations should ensure they have procedures to quickly identify and respond to such objections. A common misconception is that restriction means deletion, but it actually involves pausing processing activities. This requirement is triggered when a data subject formally objects to processing, and the organization must assess the validity of the objection before resuming processing.

title: Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing
obligation_text: The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.37.rationale: Documented operating procedures must include the process for notifying recipients when rectification, erasure or restriction has been applied to personal data shared with them.

business_description: When an organization corrects, deletes, or limits the processing of personal data, it must inform all parties who previously received that data. This obligation is triggered when changes are made under the rights of rectification, erasure, or restriction of processing. The organization must notify each recipient unless it is impossible or requires excessive effort. A common misconception is that this obligation applies to all data changes, but it only applies to those specific rights. Organizations should ensure they have a process to track data disclosures and manage notifications efficiently.

title: Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing — paragraph 1

business_description: This obligation requires data controllers to inform all parties who have received personal data about any corrections, deletions, or processing restrictions applied to that data. This is triggered when personal data is rectified, erased, or restricted under specific GDPR articles. The organization must notify each recipient unless doing so is impossible or requires disproportionate effort. A common misconception is that this obligation applies to all data changes, but it specifically relates to those changes made under Articles 16, 17(1), and 18. Controllers should have processes in place to track data disclosures and manage notifications efficiently.

title: Right to data portability
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance, where: (a) the processing is based on consent or on a contract; and (b) the processing is carried out by automated means.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.14.rationale: Information transfer controls must cover secure provision of portable personal data — format, encryption, integrity verification.

ISO27001:2022:A.8.24.rationale: Cryptography must protect portable personal data in transit — the exported dataset must be encrypted for transmission.

business_description: The right to data portability allows individuals to obtain and reuse their personal data across different services. This right is triggered when data is processed based on consent or a contract and is done through automated means. Organizations must provide the data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format, enabling easy transfer to another service provider. A common misconception is that this right applies to all data processing activities, but it only applies under specific conditions. Organizations should ensure they have systems in place to facilitate these requests efficiently and without unnecessary barriers.

title: Right to data portability — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance, where: (a) the processing is based on consent or on a contract; and (b) the processing is carried out by automated means.
business_description: The right to data portability allows individuals to obtain and reuse their personal data across different services. This right is triggered when data processing is based on the individual's consent or a contract and is conducted through automated means. Organizations must provide the data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format, enabling the individual to transfer it to another service provider without obstacles. A common misconception is that this right applies to all data processing activities, but it only applies under the specified conditions. Organizations should ensure they have systems in place to facilitate these requests efficiently.

title: Right to object
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1). The controller shall no longer process the personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject. Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.15.rationale: Access control systems must implement opt-out/objection flags that prevent processing for direct marketing and profiling.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.9.rationale: Asset inventory with purpose metadata enables identifying and stopping all processing activities the subject has objected to.

business_description: The right to object allows individuals to stop their personal data from being processed under certain conditions. This right is triggered when data processing is based on public interest or legitimate interests. If an individual objects, the organization must stop processing their data unless it can prove a compelling reason to continue. For direct marketing, individuals can object at any time, and the organization must cease processing immediately. A common misconception is that this right applies to all processing activities, but it specifically targets those based on certain legal grounds.

scenario: customer opts out of marketing emails

title: Right to object — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1). The controller shall no longer process the personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject.
business_description: This article gives individuals the right to object to the processing of their personal data when it is based on the public interest or legitimate interests of the controller. Organizations must stop processing the data unless they can prove that their reasons for processing are more important than the individual's rights and freedoms. This right is triggered when a data subject raises an objection related to their specific situation. A common misconception is that this right applies to all types of data processing, but it specifically pertains to processing under certain legal bases.

title: Right to object — paragraph 2
obligation_text: Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing.
business_description: When an organization processes personal data for direct marketing purposes, individuals have the right to object to this processing at any time. This means that if a person tells your organization to stop using their data for marketing, you must comply promptly. This right is triggered whenever personal data is used to promote products or services directly to individuals. Organizations often misunderstand this as only applying to email marketing, but it covers all forms of direct marketing. It's crucial to have processes in place to quickly honor such objections and ensure no further marketing communications are sent to the objecting individual.

title: Automated individual decision-making, including profiling
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision: (a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract; (b) is authorised by Union or Member State law; or (c) is based on the data subject's explicit consent. In the cases referred to in points (a) and (c) of paragraph 2, the data controller shall implement suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms, including at least the right to obtain human intervention, to express his or her point of view and to contest the decision.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.37.rationale: Documented operating procedures must define the process for human review of automated decisions when requested by data subjects.

ISO27001:2022:A.6.1.2.rationale: Risk assessment must evaluate risks of automated decision-making including profiling — Art.22 requires DPIA in many cases.

ISO27001:2022:A.8.9.rationale: Configuration management of automated decision systems must document the logic used for profiling and automated decisions.

business_description: Article 22 of the GDPR gives individuals the right to not be subject to decisions made solely by automated processes, like algorithms, if these decisions have significant legal or similar effects on them. This rule is triggered when an organization uses automated systems to make decisions without human involvement. Exceptions exist if the decision is necessary for a contract, authorized by law, or based on explicit consent. Organizations must ensure safeguards like allowing human review, enabling individuals to express their views, and contesting decisions. A common misconception is that all automated decisions are prohibited, but only those with significant effects are covered.

title: Automated individual decision-making, including profiling — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her.
business_description: This article ensures that individuals are not subject to decisions made solely by automated systems, such as algorithms or AI, if these decisions have significant legal or similar effects on them. Organizations must ensure that any decision impacting a person's legal rights or significantly affecting them is not made without human intervention. This requirement is triggered when a decision could affect someone's legal status, rights, or opportunities. A common misconception is that all automated decisions are prohibited, but the rule applies only when decisions are made without any human oversight and have significant impacts.

title: Automated individual decision-making, including profiling — paragraph 2
obligation_text: Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision: (a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract; (b) is authorised by Union or Member State law; or (c) is based on the data subject's explicit consent.
business_description: This article outlines exceptions to the general prohibition on automated decision-making that significantly affects individuals. Organizations can engage in such decision-making if it is necessary for a contract, authorized by law, or based on explicit consent from the individual. In practice, this means that if a decision is essential for fulfilling a contract, legally mandated, or the individual has clearly agreed to it, the organization can proceed with automation. It's crucial for organizations to document the necessity, legal basis, or consent to ensure compliance. A common misconception is that any consent suffices, but it must be explicit and informed.

title: Automated individual decision-making, including profiling — paragraph 3
obligation_text: In the cases referred to in points (a) and (c) of paragraph 2, the data controller shall implement suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and freedoms, including at least the right to obtain human intervention, to express his or her point of view and to contest the decision.
business_description: This provision is triggered when an organization uses automated decision-making systems, including profiling, in specific cases outlined in the GDPR. It requires organizations to put in place measures that protect the rights of individuals affected by these decisions. Specifically, individuals must have the opportunity to request human intervention, express their opinions, and challenge the decisions made by automated systems. This ensures that individuals are not solely subjected to decisions made by algorithms without recourse. A common misconception is that any automated decision-making triggers these obligations, but it only applies to certain cases specified in the regulation.

title: Restrictions
obligation_text: Union or Member State law may restrict by way of a legislative measure the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5, when such a restriction respects the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms and is a necessary and proportionate measure to safeguard: (a) national security; (b) public security; (c) defence; (d) the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences.
business_description: Article 23 allows EU or Member State laws to limit certain GDPR rights and obligations if necessary for reasons like national security or crime prevention. This means that in specific situations, organizations might not have to comply with requests related to data subject rights, such as access or deletion, if a legislative measure justifies it. These restrictions must be essential and balanced, ensuring they don't undermine fundamental rights. Organizations should be aware that such restrictions are not arbitrary and must be grounded in law. A common misconception is that any business need can justify these restrictions, which is not the case.

title: Restrictions — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Union or Member State law may restrict by way of a legislative measure the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5, when such a restriction respects the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms and is a necessary and proportionate measure to safeguard: (a) national security; (b) public security; (c) defence; (d) the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences.
business_description: Article 23.1 allows EU or Member State laws to limit certain data protection rights and obligations if necessary to protect national or public security, defense, or to aid in criminal investigations. This means that organizations may not always have to comply with requests related to data subject rights if a legal restriction is in place. However, these restrictions must be essential and proportionate, ensuring they do not undermine fundamental rights. Organizations should be aware of any specific laws that apply to their operations and understand when these exceptions can be legally invoked.

title: Responsibility of the controller
obligation_text: Taking into account the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risks of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure and to be able to demonstrate that processing is performed in accordance with this Regulation. Those measures shall be reviewed and updated where necessary. Where proportionate in relation to processing activities, the measures referred to in paragraph 1 shall include the implementation of appropriate data protection policies by the controller.

This means that if the nature, scope, context, and purposes of data processing warrant it, the organization must establish policies to ensure data protection. This is triggered when the processing activities are significant enough to pose risks to data subjects. Organizations often misunderstand this as a blanket requirement for all processing activities, but it is only necessary when proportionate. The focus is on tailoring policies to the specific risks and needs of the processing activities involved.

title: Data protection by design and by default
obligation_text: Taking into account the state of the art, the cost of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risks, the controller shall, both at the time of the determination of the means for processing and at the time of the processing itself, implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, such as pseudonymisation, which are designed to implement data-protection principles in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing. The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for ensuring that, by default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose of the processing are processed.

title: Joint controllers
obligation_text: Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they shall be joint controllers. They shall in a transparent manner determine their respective responsibilities for compliance with the obligations under this Regulation, in particular as regards the exercising of the rights of the data subject, by means of an arrangement between them. The arrangement referred to in paragraph 1 shall duly reflect the respective roles and relationships of the joint controllers vis-à-vis the data subjects. The essence of the arrangement shall be made available to the data subject.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.19.rationale: Information security in supplier relationships covers joint controller arrangements — security requirements and responsibilities must be defined in joint controller agreements.

ISO27001:2022:A.5.20.rationale: Supplier agreements must include joint controller arrangements with explicit data protection obligations for each party.

business_description: When two or more organizations jointly decide why and how personal data is processed, they become joint controllers. This requires them to clearly outline their respective responsibilities for GDPR compliance, especially regarding data subject rights. They must create an arrangement that reflects their roles and relationships, and the key aspects of this arrangement should be accessible to the data subjects. This ensures transparency and accountability. A common misconception is that joint controllers share equal responsibility for all compliance aspects, but responsibilities can be divided based on their roles.

title: Joint controllers — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they shall be joint controllers. They shall in a transparent manner determine their respective responsibilities for compliance with the obligations under this Regulation, in particular as regards the exercising of the rights of the data subject, by means of an arrangement between them.
business_description: When two or more organizations jointly decide why and how personal data should be processed, they are considered joint controllers. This means they must clearly define and agree on their individual responsibilities for complying with GDPR obligations. This is particularly important for managing data subject rights. The arrangement should be transparent and documented, ensuring each party knows their role in protecting personal data. A common misconception is that joint controllers share equal responsibility for all compliance aspects, but responsibilities can be divided based on the arrangement.

title: Joint controllers — paragraph 2
obligation_text: The arrangement referred to in paragraph 1 shall duly reflect the respective roles and relationships of the joint controllers vis-à-vis the data subjects. The essence of the arrangement shall be made available to the data subject.
business_description: When two or more organizations jointly determine the purposes and means of processing personal data, they are considered joint controllers. This article requires them to clearly define their respective roles and responsibilities in relation to data subjects. This agreement should be documented and its essence communicated to the data subjects, ensuring transparency. This is triggered when organizations collaborate on data processing activities. A common misconception is that joint controllers can avoid sharing this information with data subjects, but transparency is mandatory.

title: Representatives of controllers or processors not established in the Union
obligation_text: Where Article 3(2) applies, the controller or the processor shall designate in writing a representative in the Union. The representative shall be mandated by the controller or processor to be addressed in addition to or instead of the controller or the processor by supervisory authorities and data subjects on all issues related to processing for the purposes of ensuring compliance with this Regulation.
business_description: When a company outside the EU offers goods or services to people in the EU or monitors their behavior, it must appoint a representative within the EU. This representative acts as a point of contact for EU data protection authorities and individuals regarding data processing activities. The appointment must be in writing, and the representative is responsible for ensuring compliance with GDPR. A common misconception is that appointing a representative transfers all compliance obligations to them, but the original company remains fully accountable.

title: Representatives of controllers or processors not established in the Union — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Where Article 3(2) applies, the controller or the processor shall designate in writing a representative in the Union.
business_description: If your organization is a data controller or processor not based in the EU but offers goods or services to individuals in the EU or monitors their behavior, you must appoint a representative within the EU. This representative acts as a point of contact for data subjects and supervisory authorities. The requirement is triggered when your activities fall under the scope of GDPR as outlined in Article 3(2). A common misconception is that having a representative means transferring liability, but the controller or processor remains fully responsible for compliance.

title: Representatives of controllers or processors not established in the Union — paragraph 4
obligation_text: The representative shall be mandated by the controller or processor to be addressed in addition to or instead of the controller or the processor by supervisory authorities and data subjects on all issues related to processing for the purposes of ensuring compliance with this Regulation.
business_description: This article requires that if a controller or processor is not established in the EU, they must appoint a representative within the EU. This representative acts as a point of contact for EU supervisory authorities and data subjects regarding data processing activities. The representative can be addressed in addition to or instead of the controller or processor for compliance issues. This requirement is triggered when a non-EU entity processes personal data of individuals in the EU. A common misconception is that appointing a representative transfers all compliance responsibilities, which is not the case; the original entity remains responsible for compliance.

business_description: Article 29 of the GDPR mandates that any processing of personal data by a processor or anyone under the authority of the controller must be based on the controller's instructions. This means that processors cannot independently decide how to handle personal data unless a specific law requires them to do so. Organizations must ensure that all personnel involved in data processing are aware of and adhere to the controller's directives. A common misconception is that processors have more autonomy than they actually do; in reality, they must strictly follow the controller's guidance unless legally obligated otherwise.

title: Processing under the authority of the controller or processor — paragraph 1

business_description: This article mandates that any data processor or individual working under the authority of a data controller or processor can only handle personal data based on explicit instructions from the controller. This requirement is triggered whenever personal data is accessed or processed by these parties. Organizations must ensure that all personnel and processors are aware of and adhere to the controller's instructions, except where EU or Member State law requires otherwise. A common misconception is that processors have autonomy over data processing decisions, but they must strictly follow the controller's directives unless legally obligated otherwise.

title: Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject
obligation_text: When the personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller shall communicate the personal data breach to the data subject without undue delay. The communication to the data subject shall describe in clear and plain language the nature of the personal data breach and contain at least the name and contact details of the DPO, the likely consequences, and the measures taken or proposed. The communication to the data subject shall not be required if: (a) the controller has implemented appropriate protection measures making the data unintelligible such as encryption; (b) the controller has taken subsequent measures ensuring high risk is no longer likely to materialise; or (c) it would involve disproportionate effort.

title: Data protection impact assessment
obligation_text: Where a type of processing in particular using new technologies is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller shall, prior to the processing, carry out an assessment of the impact of the envisaged processing operations on the protection of personal data. A data protection impact assessment shall in particular be required in the case of: (a) a systematic and extensive evaluation based on automated processing, including profiling; (b) processing on a large scale of special categories of data; or (c) a systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area on a large scale. The assessment shall contain at least: (a) a systematic description of the envisaged processing operations and the purposes; (b) an assessment of the necessity and proportionality; (c) an assessment of the risks; and (d) the measures envisaged to address the risks.

business_description: A data protection impact assessment (DPIA) is necessary when certain high-risk processing activities are planned. This includes when an organization conducts systematic and extensive evaluations using automated processing, processes large volumes of sensitive data, or monitors public areas on a large scale. The DPIA helps identify and mitigate risks to individuals' privacy. Organizations often misunderstand DPIAs as optional, but they are mandatory in these scenarios. Conducting a DPIA involves assessing the necessity and proportionality of the processing, identifying risks, and implementing measures to address them. It is a proactive step to ensure compliance with data protection laws.

title: Prior consultation
obligation_text: The controller shall consult the supervisory authority prior to processing where a DPIA under Article 35 indicates that the processing would result in a high residual risk in the absence of measures taken by the controller to mitigate the risk.
business_description: Article 36 requires organizations to consult with their supervisory authority before processing personal data if a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) reveals a high residual risk that cannot be mitigated. This is triggered when the DPIA, conducted under Article 35, identifies significant risks to data subjects that remain even after implementing risk-reduction measures. Organizations must seek guidance from the authority to ensure compliance and protect data subjects' rights. A common misconception is that this consultation is optional; however, it is mandatory when high risks are identified and unmitigated.

obligation_text: The controller shall consult the supervisory authority prior to processing where a DPIA under Article 35 indicates that the processing would result in a high residual risk in the absence of measures taken by the controller to mitigate the risk.

This step is crucial to ensure that the risks are properly managed and that the rights of data subjects are protected. It is triggered when a DPIA identifies high residual risks despite mitigation efforts. A common misconception is that any high-risk processing requires consultation, but it is specifically when those risks remain unmitigated that consultation is necessary.

title: Designation of the data protection officer
obligation_text: The controller and the processor shall designate a data protection officer in any case where: (a) the processing is carried out by a public authority; (b) the core activities require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale; or (c) the core activities consist of processing on a large scale of special categories of data. The data protection officer shall be designated on the basis of professional qualities and, in particular, expert knowledge of data protection law and practices. The controller or processor shall publish the contact details of the data protection officer and communicate them to the supervisory authority.

title: Position of the data protection officer

The DPO should report directly to the highest level of management. A common misconception is that the DPO can be directed by management on how to perform their tasks, which is not allowed. title: Tasks of the data protection officer
obligation_text: The data protection officer shall have at least the following tasks: (a) to inform and advise the controller or the processor and the employees of their obligations pursuant to this Regulation; (b) to monitor compliance with this Regulation; (c) to provide advice where requested as regards the DPIA and monitor its performance; (d) to cooperate with the supervisory authority; (e) to act as the contact point for the supervisory authority on issues relating to processing.
business_description: The role of the Data Protection Officer (DPO) is crucial for ensuring that an organization complies with GDPR. When an organization processes personal data, the DPO must inform and advise the company and its staff about their GDPR obligations. The DPO also monitors compliance, advises on Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), and ensures these assessments are carried out properly. Additionally, the DPO acts as a liaison with the supervisory authority, facilitating communication and cooperation. Organizations often misunderstand the DPO's role as purely advisory, but it also includes active monitoring and engagement with regulatory bodies.

title: Codes of conduct
obligation_text: The Member States, the supervisory authorities, the Board and the Commission shall encourage the drawing up of codes of conduct intended to contribute to the proper application of this Regulation, taking account of the specific features of the various processing sectors and the specific needs of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises.
business_description: Article 40 of the GDPR encourages the creation of codes of conduct to help ensure the regulation is applied correctly across different sectors. This is particularly relevant for industries with unique processing activities or for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises that may have specific needs. Organizations are encouraged to collaborate with supervisory authorities and other stakeholders to develop these codes. The goal is to provide practical guidance tailored to specific industry contexts, helping organizations comply with GDPR requirements more effectively. A common misconception is that these codes are mandatory, but they are actually voluntary and meant to aid compliance efforts.

title: Codes of conduct — paragraph 1

These codes can provide tailored guidance and best practices, making compliance more manageable. It is important to note that while these codes are encouraged, they are not mandatory. A common misconception is that adhering to a code of conduct is equivalent to full compliance with the GDPR, which is not the case.

title: Monitoring of approved codes of conduct

The monitoring must be conducted by a body with the necessary expertise and accreditation from the relevant supervisory authority. This means that organizations need to ensure that any monitoring body they engage is properly accredited and knowledgeable about the specific code of conduct. title: Transfers on the basis of an adequacy decision

title: Binding corporate rules
obligation_text: The competent supervisory authority shall approve binding corporate rules provided that they: (a) are legally binding and apply to and are enforced by every member of the group; (b) expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects; and (c) fulfil the requirements laid down in paragraph 2.
business_description: Binding corporate rules (BCRs) are internal policies adopted by multinational companies to allow the transfer of personal data across borders within the same corporate group. They are triggered when a company wants to transfer data internationally and must be approved by a supervisory authority. BCRs must be legally binding, enforceable, and grant rights to data subjects. Companies often misunderstand BCRs as optional or think they apply to external partners, but they are specifically for intra-group transfers. Organizations must ensure BCRs are comprehensive and meet specific legal requirements to gain approval.

title: Binding corporate rules — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The competent supervisory authority shall approve binding corporate rules provided that they: (a) are legally binding and apply to and are enforced by every member of the group; (b) expressly confer enforceable rights on data subjects; and (c) fulfil the requirements laid down in paragraph 2.
business_description: Binding corporate rules (BCRs) are internal policies adopted by multinational companies to allow the transfer of personal data across borders within the same corporate group. This article is triggered when a company seeks to transfer personal data internationally within its group. The rules must be legally binding, applicable to all group members, and enforceable. They must also grant enforceable rights to individuals whose data is being transferred. Approval from the relevant supervisory authority is required, ensuring the BCRs meet specific criteria. A common misconception is that BCRs are optional; however, they are essential for lawful data transfers within a corporate group.

title: Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law
obligation_text: Any judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data may only be recognised or enforceable in any manner if based on an international agreement in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State.
business_description: This article applies when a court or administrative authority from outside the EU requests a company to transfer or disclose personal data. It requires that such requests can only be fulfilled if there is an existing international agreement between the EU (or an EU Member State) and the requesting country. This means that companies cannot comply with these requests based solely on the foreign authority's order. A common misconception is that any foreign legal request must be complied with, but without an international agreement, such requests are not enforceable under GDPR.

title: Derogations for specific situations
obligation_text: In the absence of an adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards, a transfer of personal data to a third country shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract; (c) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (d) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims.
business_description: Article 49 of the GDPR allows for the transfer of personal data to countries without adequate data protection if specific conditions are met. This is relevant when there is no adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards in place. Organizations can transfer data if the individual has given explicit consent, if it's necessary for a contract, for public interest reasons, or for legal claims. It's crucial for controllers and processors to document the justification for such transfers and ensure they meet one of these conditions. title: Supervisory authority

title: Independence
obligation_text: Each supervisory authority shall act with complete independence in performing its tasks and exercising its powers. title: General conditions for the members of the supervisory authority
obligation_text: Member States shall provide for each member of their supervisory authorities to be appointed by means of a transparent procedure by their parliament, government, head of State or an independent body entrusted with the appointment under Member State law.

title: Competence
obligation_text: Each supervisory authority shall be competent for the performance of the tasks assigned to and the exercise of the powers conferred on it in accordance with this Regulation on the territory of its own Member State.
business_description: Article 55 of the GDPR specifies that each supervisory authority is responsible for overseeing GDPR compliance within its own Member State. Organizations must ensure they understand which supervisory authority they are accountable to and comply with any local variations in enforcement. title: Competence — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Each supervisory authority shall be competent for the performance of the tasks assigned to and the exercise of the powers conferred on it in accordance with this Regulation on the territory of its own Member State.

title: Competence of the lead supervisory authority
obligation_text: Without prejudice to Article 55, the supervisory authority of the main establishment or of the single establishment of the controller or processor shall be competent to act as lead supervisory authority for the cross-border processing carried out by that controller or processor.
business_description: Article 56 of the GDPR determines which supervisory authority is responsible for overseeing cross-border data processing activities. This is relevant when a company operates in multiple EU countries. The lead supervisory authority is the one in the country where the company has its main or single establishment. This means that for cross-border data processing, the company will primarily interact with this authority for compliance matters. A common misconception is that companies must deal with multiple authorities for cross-border processing, but Article 56 centralizes this to one lead authority, simplifying compliance efforts.

title: Competence of the lead supervisory authority — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Without prejudice to Article 55, the supervisory authority of the main establishment or of the single establishment of the controller or processor shall be competent to act as lead supervisory authority for the cross-border processing carried out by that controller or processor.
business_description: Article 56.1 of the GDPR determines which supervisory authority is responsible for overseeing cross-border data processing activities. title: Tasks
obligation_text: Without prejudice to other tasks set out under this Regulation, each supervisory authority shall on its territory monitor and enforce the application of this Regulation; promote public awareness and understanding of the risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to processing; advise the national parliament, government and other institutions on legislative and administrative measures.
business_description: Article 57 outlines the responsibilities of supervisory authorities, which are organizations tasked with ensuring GDPR compliance within their jurisdiction. Organizations should be aware that supervisory authorities also work to educate the public and advise government bodies on data protection matters. A common misconception is that supervisory authorities only enforce penalties, but they also play a key role in promoting awareness and understanding of data protection rights and responsibilities.

title: Tasks — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Without prejudice to other tasks set out under this Regulation, each supervisory authority shall on its territory monitor and enforce the application of this Regulation; promote public awareness and understanding of the risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to processing; advise the national parliament, government and other institutions on legislative and administrative measures.

title: Powers
obligation_text: Each supervisory authority shall have investigative powers, corrective powers, authorisation and advisory powers and the power to bring infringements of this Regulation to the attention of judicial authorities, including the power to impose administrative fines pursuant to Article 83.
business_description: Article 58 of the GDPR outlines the powers of supervisory authorities, which include the ability to investigate, correct, authorize, and advise on data protection matters. For organizations, this means that if a supervisory authority believes there is a potential infringement of GDPR, they can conduct investigations and impose corrective measures, such as fines. Organizations must cooperate with these authorities and ensure compliance to avoid penalties. A common misconception is that supervisory authorities only have the power to impose fines, but they also provide guidance and support to help organizations comply with GDPR requirements.

title: Powers — paragraph 1

business_description: Article 58.1 of the GDPR outlines the powers granted to supervisory authorities. title: Activity reports
obligation_text: Each supervisory authority shall draw up an annual report on its activities, which may include a list of types of infringement notified and types of measures taken in accordance with Article 58(2).
business_description: Article 59 requires each supervisory authority to create an annual report detailing their activities over the year. title: Activity reports — paragraph 1

title: Cooperation between the lead supervisory authority and the other supervisory authorities concerned
obligation_text: The lead supervisory authority shall cooperate with the other supervisory authorities concerned in an endeavour to reach consensus. As part of the cooperation, the lead supervisory authority shall submit the relevant information on the matter to the other supervisory authorities concerned and shall present a draft decision to them.
business_description: Article 60 of the GDPR requires the lead supervisory authority to work collaboratively with other relevant supervisory authorities to reach a consensus on decisions related to data protection matters. title: Cooperation between the lead supervisory authority and the other supervisory authorities concerned — paragraph 1

title: Mutual assistance
obligation_text: Supervisory authorities shall provide each other with relevant information and mutual assistance in order to implement and apply this Regulation in a consistent manner, and shall put in place measures for effective cooperation with one another.
business_description: Article 61 requires supervisory authorities across different jurisdictions to share relevant information and assist each other to ensure the GDPR is applied consistently. title: Mutual assistance — paragraph 1

title: Joint operations of supervisory authorities
obligation_text: The supervisory authorities shall, where appropriate, conduct joint operations including joint investigations and joint enforcement measures in which members or staff of supervisory authorities of other Member States are involved.

title: Consistency mechanism
obligation_text: In order to contribute to the consistent application of this Regulation throughout the Union, the supervisory authorities shall cooperate with each other and, where relevant, with the Commission, through the consistency mechanism as set out in this Section.
business_description: The consistency mechanism ensures that data protection laws are applied uniformly across the EU. It is triggered when supervisory authorities need to collaborate on cross-border data protection issues. For controllers or processors, this means that if your data processing activities affect multiple EU countries, the relevant supervisory authorities will work together to ensure consistent enforcement of GDPR. Organizations are not directly involved in this process but should be aware that it may influence how their compliance is assessed. title: Consistency mechanism — paragraph 1
obligation_text: In order to contribute to the consistent application of this Regulation throughout the Union, the supervisory authorities shall cooperate with each other and, where relevant, with the Commission, through the consistency mechanism as set out in this Section.

title: Opinion of the Board
obligation_text: The Board shall issue an opinion where a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt measures in cases where the draft measure relates to processing activities which are related to the offering of goods or services to data subjects in several Member States.
business_description: Article 64 of the GDPR requires the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) to provide an opinion when a supervisory authority plans to implement measures affecting data processing activities that involve offering goods or services across multiple EU Member States. This is triggered when a draft measure could impact data subjects in more than one country. Organizations must be aware that such measures require consultation with the Board to ensure consistency across the EU. A common misconception is that this opinion is optional; however, it is a mandatory step in the process to ensure harmonized data protection practices.

title: Opinion of the Board — paragraph 1

title: Dispute resolution by the Board
obligation_text: In order to ensure the correct and consistent application of this Regulation in individual cases, the Board shall adopt a binding decision in the cases referred to in this article where there are conflicting views among supervisory authorities.
business_description: Article 65 of the GDPR is relevant when there is a disagreement between supervisory authorities about how to apply the regulation in a specific case. When such conflicts arise, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) steps in to make a binding decision to resolve the dispute. This ensures that the GDPR is applied consistently across different jurisdictions. For organizations, this means that if they are involved in a case where supervisory authorities disagree, they must comply with the decision made by the EDPB. title: Urgency procedure
obligation_text: In exceptional circumstances, where a supervisory authority concerned considers that there is an urgent need to act in order to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects, it may immediately adopt provisional measures intended to produce legal effects on its own territory with a specified period of validity.
business_description: Article 66 of the GDPR allows a supervisory authority to take immediate action in urgent situations to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects. This is triggered when there is an exceptional circumstance that requires swift intervention. For controllers and processors, this means they may be subject to provisional measures imposed by the authority, which are legally binding within the authority's jurisdiction for a specified time. Organizations should be prepared to comply quickly with such measures. A common misconception is that these measures apply EU-wide, but they are limited to the territory of the issuing authority.

title: Urgency procedure — paragraph 1

title: Exchange of information
obligation_text: The Commission may adopt implementing acts of general scope in order to specify the arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities, and between supervisory authorities and the Board.

title: European Data Protection Board
obligation_text: The European Data Protection Board (the Board) is hereby established as a body of the Union and shall have legal personality. The Board shall be represented by its Chair.

title: European Data Protection Board — paragraph 1

title: Independence
obligation_text: The Board shall act independently when performing its tasks or exercising its powers pursuant to Articles 70 and 71. The Board shall neither seek nor take instructions from anybody.

title: Independence — paragraph 1

title: Tasks of the Board
obligation_text: The Board shall ensure the consistent application of this Regulation. To that end, the Board shall issue guidelines, recommendations, and best practices in order to encourage consistent application of this Regulation.

title: Tasks of the Board — paragraph 1

title: Reports
obligation_text: The Board shall draw up an annual report regarding the protection of natural persons with regard to processing in the Union and, where relevant, in third countries and international organisations.

title: Reports — paragraph 1
