title: Procedure
obligation_text: The Board shall take decisions by a simple majority of its members, unless otherwise provided for in this Regulation. The Board shall adopt its own rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority of its members.
business_description: Article 72 outlines how the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) makes decisions. For most decisions, a simple majority of the Board members is needed. However, when it comes to adopting its own rules of procedure, a two-thirds majority is required. This means that controllers and processors should be aware that the Board's decisions are made collectively and that the rules governing these decisions are established with a higher level of consensus. This article is relevant when understanding how the EDPB's decisions, which can impact data protection practices, are formulated and agreed upon.

title: Procedure — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The Board shall take decisions by a simple majority of its members, unless otherwise provided for in this Regulation. The Board shall adopt its own rules of procedure by a two-thirds majority of its members.
business_description: This article outlines how the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) makes decisions. For controllers and processors, this means understanding that the EDPB's decisions are typically made by a simple majority vote among its members. However, when it comes to adopting its own rules of procedure, a two-thirds majority is required. This is relevant when the EDPB issues guidelines or decisions that may impact your data processing activities. Organizations should be aware that these decisions can influence compliance requirements and should monitor EDPB outputs for any changes that may affect their operations.

title: Chair
obligation_text: The Board shall elect a chair and two deputy chairs from amongst its members by simple majority. The term of office shall be five years and shall be renewable once.
business_description: This article outlines the process for electing leadership within the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). The Board is responsible for electing a chair and two deputy chairs from its members through a simple majority vote. Each elected position has a term of five years, with the possibility of one renewal. This process is primarily internal to the EDPB and does not directly impose obligations on controllers or processors. However, understanding the leadership structure can help organizations anticipate how decisions and guidance from the EDPB might be influenced. A common misconception is that this election process affects the roles within individual organizations, which it does not.

title: Chair — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The Board shall elect a chair and two deputy chairs from amongst its members by simple majority. The term of office shall be five years and shall be renewable once.
business_description: This article outlines the process for selecting leadership within the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). It requires the Board to elect a chair and two deputy chairs from its members through a simple majority vote. This leadership structure is essential for guiding the Board's activities and ensuring effective decision-making. The election occurs every five years, with the possibility of re-election for one additional term. This process is internal to the Board and does not directly impose obligations on data controllers or processors, but understanding the Board's leadership can help organizations anticipate regulatory priorities and actions.

title: Tasks of the Chair
obligation_text: The Chair shall convene the meetings of the Board and prepare its agenda; notify decisions adopted by the Board to the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned; ensure the timely performance of the tasks of the Board.
business_description: Article 74 outlines the responsibilities of the Chair of the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). In practice, this means that the Chair is responsible for organizing and leading the Board's meetings, setting the agenda, and ensuring that decisions made by the Board are communicated to the relevant supervisory authorities. This article is triggered when the Board needs to convene to discuss data protection matters. Organizations should be aware that the Chair plays a crucial role in coordinating the Board's activities, but this does not impose direct obligations on controllers or processors.

title: Tasks of the Chair — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The Chair shall convene the meetings of the Board and prepare its agenda; notify decisions adopted by the Board to the lead supervisory authority and supervisory authorities concerned; ensure the timely performance of the tasks of the Board.
business_description: This article outlines the responsibilities of the Chair of the European Data Protection Board (EDPB). In practice, it means that the Chair is responsible for organizing and setting the agenda for Board meetings. The Chair must also communicate decisions made by the Board to the relevant supervisory authorities, ensuring that these decisions are implemented effectively. This process is triggered when the Board needs to meet to discuss data protection issues or make decisions. Organizations should be aware that the Chair's role is crucial for the smooth operation of the Board, but they are not directly responsible for these tasks themselves.

title: Secretariat
obligation_text: The Board shall have a secretariat, which shall be provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor. The secretariat shall perform its tasks exclusively under the instructions of the Chair of the Board.
business_description: Article 75 of the GDPR establishes that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) will have a secretariat provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS). This secretariat operates solely under the direction of the Chair of the Board. For controllers and processors, this means that the EDPB's administrative and logistical support is centralized and managed by the EDPS, ensuring consistency in the Board's operations. Organizations are not directly affected by this article in terms of compliance actions, but understanding the structure can help in navigating interactions with the EDPB.

title: Secretariat — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The Board shall have a secretariat, which shall be provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor. The secretariat shall perform its tasks exclusively under the instructions of the Chair of the Board.
business_description: Article 75.1 outlines that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) must have a secretariat, which is provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS). This secretariat operates under the direction of the Chair of the Board. For controllers and processors, this means that the EDPB's administrative and logistical functions are managed by the EDPS, ensuring consistency and coordination in data protection matters across the EU. Organizations do not have direct obligations under this article, but understanding the structure helps in knowing how data protection guidance and decisions are coordinated at the EU level.

title: Confidentiality
obligation_text: The discussions of the Board shall be confidential where the Board deems it necessary, as provided for in its rules of procedure. Access to documents submitted to the Board shall be governed by Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001.
business_description: Article 76 of the GDPR requires that discussions within the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) remain confidential when deemed necessary by the Board itself. This means that any sensitive deliberations or decisions made by the Board are not to be disclosed publicly unless specified otherwise. For organizations, this means that any documents submitted to the Board are subject to confidentiality rules and are accessed according to specific regulations. This is triggered when an organization is involved in proceedings or consultations with the Board. A common misconception is that all Board discussions are public, but confidentiality is maintained as needed.

title: Confidentiality — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The discussions of the Board shall be confidential where the Board deems it necessary, as provided for in its rules of procedure. Access to documents submitted to the Board shall be governed by Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001.
business_description: This article means that when the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) holds discussions, these discussions are kept confidential if the Board decides it's necessary. This confidentiality is based on the Board's internal rules. For organizations, this means that any documents they submit to the Board are subject to access rules under a separate regulation, specifically Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001. This is triggered when an organization is involved in proceedings with the Board. Organizations should ensure that they understand when their submissions might be accessed by others and under what conditions confidentiality applies.

title: Right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority
obligation_text: Without prejudice to any other administrative or judicial remedy, every data subject shall have the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority, in particular in the Member State of his or her habitual residence, place of work or place of the alleged infringement if the data subject considers that the processing of personal data relating to him or her infringes this Regulation.
business_description: Article 77 of the GDPR allows individuals, known as data subjects, to file a complaint with a supervisory authority if they believe their personal data is being processed in violation of GDPR. This right is activated when a data subject feels their data rights have been infringed, and it requires organizations to be prepared for potential investigations by these authorities. Organizations should ensure they have processes to handle such complaints efficiently and transparently. A common misconception is that this right only applies in the country where the organization is based, but it actually applies in the data subject's country of residence, work, or where the infringement occurred.

title: Right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Without prejudice to any other administrative or judicial remedy, every data subject shall have the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority, in particular in the Member State of his or her habitual residence, place of work or place of the alleged infringement if the data subject considers that the processing of personal data relating to him or her infringes this Regulation.
business_description: This article grants individuals the right to file a complaint with a data protection authority if they believe their personal data is being mishandled in violation of GDPR. This right is activated when a data subject feels their data is being processed unlawfully. Organizations must be prepared to respond to inquiries from supervisory authorities and facilitate the complaint process. A common misconception is that complaints can only be lodged in the country where the organization is based, but they can also be filed in the individual's country of residence or where the alleged infringement occurred.

title: Right to an effective judicial remedy against a supervisory authority
obligation_text: Without prejudice to any other administrative or non-judicial remedy, each natural or legal person shall have the right to an effective judicial remedy against a legally binding decision of a supervisory authority concerning them.
business_description: Article 78 of the GDPR ensures that individuals or organizations can challenge decisions made by data protection authorities in court. This right is triggered when a supervisory authority issues a legally binding decision that affects a person or entity. Organizations must be prepared to respond to such decisions and understand that they have the option to seek judicial review if they disagree with the authority's ruling. A common misconception is that this right only applies to individuals, but it also extends to legal entities. Organizations should be aware of this right and consider it as part of their compliance strategy.

title: Right to an effective judicial remedy against a supervisory authority — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Without prejudice to any other administrative or non-judicial remedy, each natural or legal person shall have the right to an effective judicial remedy against a legally binding decision of a supervisory authority concerning them.
business_description: This article ensures that individuals or organizations have the right to challenge a decision made by a data protection supervisory authority if it legally affects them. It is triggered when a supervisory authority issues a binding decision that impacts a natural or legal person. Organizations must be prepared to facilitate this process by understanding the decisions that can be contested and ensuring they have mechanisms in place to respond to any legal challenges. A common misconception is that this right only applies to individuals, but it also extends to legal entities such as companies.

dispute over a data breach decision

title: Right to an effective judicial remedy against a controller or processor
obligation_text: Without prejudice to any available administrative or non-judicial remedy, including the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority pursuant to Article 77, each data subject shall have the right to an effective judicial remedy where he or she considers that his or her rights under this Regulation have been infringed as a result of the processing of his or her personal data.
business_description: Article 79 of the GDPR provides data subjects with the right to seek a judicial remedy if they believe their rights under the GDPR have been violated due to the processing of their personal data. This means that if an individual feels that a company has mishandled their data, they can take the matter to court. Organizations must be prepared to respond to such legal actions and ensure their data processing activities comply with GDPR standards to avoid litigation. A common misconception is that this right can be bypassed by resolving issues through a supervisory authority, but judicial remedies are always available to data subjects.

title: Right to an effective judicial remedy against a controller or processor — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Without prejudice to any available administrative or non-judicial remedy, including the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority pursuant to Article 77, each data subject shall have the right to an effective judicial remedy where he or she considers that his or her rights under this Regulation have been infringed as a result of the processing of his or her personal data.
business_description: Article 79.1 of the GDPR gives individuals the right to seek a judicial remedy if they believe their data protection rights have been violated by a data controller or processor. This is triggered when a data subject feels their personal data has been mishandled or their rights under the GDPR have been infringed. Organizations must be prepared to address such claims and potentially defend their data processing activities in court. A common misconception is that this right replaces administrative remedies, but it actually complements them, allowing individuals to pursue both avenues if necessary.

scenario: failure to comply with data subject request

title: Representation of data subjects
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to mandate a not-for-profit body, organisation or association which has been properly constituted in accordance with the law of a Member State and is active in the field of the protection of data subjects' rights to lodge the complaint on his or her behalf.
business_description: Article 80 of the GDPR allows individuals to authorize a non-profit organization to file a data protection complaint on their behalf. This is relevant for controllers and processors when they receive a complaint from such an organization. It requires them to treat the complaint as if it were directly from the data subject. This provision is triggered when a data subject chooses to exercise their rights through a third-party organization. A common misconception is that this allows any organization to represent data subjects, but it must be a non-profit active in data protection and properly constituted under national law.

scenario: data subject authorizes a non-profit to file a complaint
organization receives a complaint from a non-profit
data protection NGO files on behalf of an individual

title: Representation of data subjects — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The data subject shall have the right to mandate a not-for-profit body, organisation or association which has been properly constituted in accordance with the law of a Member State and is active in the field of the protection of data subjects' rights to lodge the complaint on his or her behalf.
business_description: Article 80.1 of the GDPR allows data subjects to appoint a not-for-profit organization to act on their behalf in lodging complaints about data protection violations. This means that if an individual believes their data rights have been infringed, they can authorize a qualified organization to represent them. For controllers and processors, this implies that they may receive complaints not only directly from individuals but also from these organizations. Organizations must be prepared to handle such representations and ensure they have processes in place to respond appropriately. A common misconception is that only individuals can lodge complaints, but this article clarifies that authorized bodies can do so as well.

title: Suspension of proceedings
obligation_text: Where a competent court of a Member State has information on proceedings, concerning the same subject matter as regards processing by the same controller or processor, that are pending in a court in another Member State, it shall contact that court in the other Member State to confirm the existence of such proceedings.
business_description: Article 81 of the GDPR is relevant when a court in one EU Member State becomes aware of ongoing legal proceedings in another Member State concerning the same data processing issue by the same controller or processor. In such cases, the court must reach out to the court in the other Member State to verify the existence of these proceedings. This ensures that there is coordination and awareness between courts handling similar cases, preventing conflicting judgments. Organizations should be aware that this article is triggered when there are parallel legal proceedings in different Member States concerning the same data processing activities.

scenario: data breach litigation in multiple countries

title: Suspension of proceedings — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Where a competent court of a Member State has information on proceedings, concerning the same subject matter as regards processing by the same controller or processor, that are pending in a court in another Member State, it shall contact that court in the other Member State to confirm the existence of such proceedings.
business_description: This article applies when a court in one EU Member State becomes aware of legal proceedings related to data processing by the same entity that are already underway in another Member State. In such cases, the court must reach out to the court in the other Member State to verify the existence of these proceedings. This ensures coordination and avoids conflicting judgments. Organizations should be aware that if they are involved in cross-border data processing litigation, courts may communicate with each other to confirm parallel cases. This does not impose direct obligations on controllers or processors but affects how their cases might be handled by the courts.

scenario: court case involving same data processing

title: Right to compensation and liability
obligation_text: Any person who has suffered material or non-material damage as a result of an infringement of this Regulation shall have the right to receive compensation from the controller or processor for the damage suffered. Any controller involved in processing shall be liable for the damage caused by processing which infringes this Regulation. A processor shall be liable for the damage caused by processing only where it has not complied with obligations of this Regulation specifically directed to processors.
business_description: Article 82 of the GDPR establishes that individuals who suffer harm due to a breach of the regulation are entitled to compensation from the responsible data controller or processor. This is triggered when a data breach or non-compliance with GDPR obligations results in material or non-material damage to an individual. Organizations must be prepared to address claims and potentially compensate affected individuals. A common misconception is that only controllers are liable, but processors can also be held accountable if they fail to meet their specific obligations under the GDPR.

scenario: data breach resulting in identity theft
unauthorized data access causing financial loss
failure to implement required security measures

title: Right to compensation and liability — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Any person who has suffered material or non-material damage as a result of an infringement of this Regulation shall have the right to receive compensation from the controller or processor for the damage suffered.
business_description: Under GDPR Article 82.1, if an individual suffers harm due to a breach of the regulation, they can claim compensation from the organization responsible. This applies to both material and non-material damages. Organizations must be prepared to address claims and potentially compensate affected individuals. This obligation is triggered when a breach occurs that causes harm, requiring organizations to assess and respond to claims appropriately. A common misconception is that only financial losses are compensable, but non-material damages like distress are also covered.

title: Right to compensation and liability — paragraph 2
obligation_text: Any controller involved in processing shall be liable for the damage caused by processing which infringes this Regulation. A processor shall be liable for the damage caused by processing only where it has not complied with obligations of this Regulation specifically directed to processors.
business_description: This article outlines the liability of controllers and processors when data processing activities violate GDPR and cause harm. Controllers are always liable for damages resulting from their processing activities. Processors, however, are only liable if they fail to meet specific GDPR obligations applicable to them. This means that if a processor follows all their specific obligations, they may not be held liable for damages. Organizations must ensure they understand their role as either a controller or processor and comply with relevant GDPR requirements to avoid liability. A common misconception is that processors are always liable for any data breach, which is not the case if they have complied with their obligations.

title: General conditions for imposing administrative fines
obligation_text: Infringements of the following provisions shall be subject to administrative fines up to 10 000 000 EUR, or in the case of an undertaking, up to 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover: (a) the obligations of the controller and the processor pursuant to Articles 8, 11, 25 to 39, 42 and 43. Infringements of the following provisions shall be subject to administrative fines up to 20 000 000 EUR, or up to 4% of total worldwide annual turnover: (a) the basic principles for processing pursuant to Articles 5, 6, 7 and 9; (b) the data subjects' rights pursuant to Articles 12 to 22; (c) the transfers of personal data pursuant to Articles 44 to 49.
evidence_requirements: Compliance programme demonstrating systematic implementation of GDPR obligations
Evidence of data protection measures taken prior to any regulatory investigation

business_description: Article 83 of the GDPR outlines the financial penalties for non-compliance with specific provisions. Organizations, whether controllers or processors, can face fines up to 10 million EUR or 2% of their global turnover for breaches related to operational obligations, such as data protection by design and default, and security measures. More severe breaches, like violating basic data processing principles or data subject rights, can lead to fines up to 20 million EUR or 4% of turnover. This article is triggered when an organization fails to comply with GDPR requirements, emphasizing the importance of adhering to data protection principles and respecting data subjects' rights.

title: General conditions for imposing administrative fines — paragraph 4
obligation_text: Infringements of the following provisions shall be subject to administrative fines up to 10 000 000 EUR, or in the case of an undertaking, up to 2% of the total worldwide annual turnover: (a) the obligations of the controller and the processor pursuant to Articles 8, 11, 25 to 39, 42 and 43.

The maximum fine is 10 million EUR or 2% of global annual turnover - whichever is higher. This tier covers procedural and organisational failures; the higher Art.83.5 tier applies to violations of fundamental principles and data subject rights.

business_description: This article outlines the severe financial penalties that can be imposed on organizations for violating key GDPR provisions. Organizations must ensure they adhere to these core principles to avoid fines that can reach up to 20 million EUR or 4% of their global annual revenue. A common misconception is that only large breaches result in fines, but even smaller violations can lead to significant penalties if they involve these critical areas.

title: Penalties
obligation_text: Member States shall lay down the rules on other penalties applicable to infringements of this Regulation in particular for infringements which are not subject to administrative fines pursuant to Article 83, and shall take all measures necessary to ensure that they are implemented. Such penalties shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive.
business_description: Article 84 requires EU Member States to establish rules for penalties related to GDPR violations that do not involve administrative fines under Article 83. Organizations must be aware that even if an infringement doesn't lead to an administrative fine, other penalties may still apply. These penalties must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. This means that businesses should ensure compliance with all aspects of the GDPR, not just those that carry fines, as other penalties can still have significant impacts. A common misconception is that only administrative fines are enforceable, but this article clarifies that other penalties can also be imposed.

title: Penalties — paragraph 1

Such penalties shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive.
business_description: Article 84.1 requires EU Member States to establish rules for penalties related to GDPR violations that do not fall under administrative fines outlined in Article 83. This means that if an organization breaches the GDPR in a way that isn't covered by the standard fines, the Member State must have other penalties in place. These penalties should be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive. Organizations need to be aware that non-compliance can lead to various penalties, not just fines, and should ensure they understand the specific rules in each Member State where they operate.

title: Processing and freedom of expression and information
obligation_text: Member States shall by law reconcile the right to the protection of personal data pursuant to this Regulation with the right to freedom of expression and information, including processing for journalistic purposes and the purposes of academic, artistic or literary expression. For processing carried out for journalistic purposes or the purpose of academic artistic or literary expression, Member States shall provide for exemptions or derogations from the provisions of this Regulation if they are necessary to reconcile the right to the protection of personal data with the freedom of expression and information.

business_description: Article 85 of the GDPR requires Member States to balance the right to personal data protection with freedom of expression and information. This is particularly relevant for activities like journalism, academia, art, and literature. When these activities involve processing personal data, Member States may allow exemptions from certain GDPR provisions to ensure freedom of expression is not unduly restricted. Organizations involved in these fields should be aware of national laws that might provide such exemptions and ensure they comply with them. A common misconception is that all GDPR obligations are waived for journalistic purposes, but exemptions are only applicable when necessary to balance these rights.

title: Processing and public access to official documents
obligation_text: Personal data in official documents held by a public authority or a public body may be disclosed by the authority or body in accordance with Union or Member State law in order to reconcile public access to official documents with the right to the protection of personal data pursuant to this Regulation.

title: Processing of the national identification number

title: Processing in the context of employment
obligation_text: Member States may, by law or by collective agreements, provide for more specific rules to ensure the protection of the rights and freedoms in respect of the processing of employees' personal data in the employment context, in particular for the purposes of the recruitment, the performance of the contract of employment, management, planning and organisation of work, equality and diversity in the workplace, health and safety at work.

Organizations must comply with these national rules, which may vary, to protect employees' rights and freedoms. A common misconception is that GDPR alone governs employee data processing, but national laws may impose additional requirements.

title: Safeguards and derogations relating to processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes
obligation_text: Processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall be subject to appropriate safeguards, in accordance with this Regulation, for the rights and freedoms of the data subject. Those safeguards shall ensure that technical and organisational measures are in place in particular in order to ensure respect for the principle of data minimisation.
business_description: Article 89 of the GDPR requires organizations processing personal data for archiving in the public interest, scientific or historical research, or statistical purposes to implement safeguards that protect the rights and freedoms of individuals. This is triggered when data is used for these specific purposes. Organizations must ensure they have technical and organizational measures in place, focusing on data minimization, which means only collecting and retaining data necessary for these purposes. A common misconception is that these activities are exempt from GDPR, but they must still comply with its principles, particularly regarding data protection and minimization.

title: Safeguards and derogations relating to processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall be subject to appropriate safeguards, in accordance with this Regulation, for the rights and freedoms of the data subject. Those safeguards shall ensure that technical and organisational measures are in place in particular in order to ensure respect for the principle of data minimisation.
business_description: This article applies when personal data is processed for archiving in the public interest, scientific or historical research, or statistical purposes. Organizations must implement appropriate safeguards to protect the rights and freedoms of individuals whose data is being processed. This means ensuring that technical and organizational measures are in place, particularly focusing on data minimization. A common misconception is that these activities are exempt from GDPR requirements, but safeguards are still necessary to ensure compliance.

title: Obligations of secrecy
obligation_text: Member States may adopt specific rules to set out the powers of the supervisory authorities in relation to controllers or processors that are subject to an obligation of professional secrecy or other equivalent obligations of secrecy where this is necessary and proportionate to reconcile the right of the protection of personal data with the obligation of secrecy.
business_description: Article 90 allows EU Member States to create specific rules for how data protection authorities can interact with organizations bound by professional secrecy, like lawyers or doctors. This is triggered when a controller or processor is subject to confidentiality obligations. Organizations must balance data protection rights with these secrecy obligations, ensuring that they comply with both. A common misconception is that data protection laws override all secrecy obligations, but this article clarifies that both must be reconciled. Organizations should be aware of their national laws that may impose additional requirements in these situations.

title: Obligations of secrecy — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Member States may adopt specific rules to set out the powers of the supervisory authorities in relation to controllers or processors that are subject to an obligation of professional secrecy or other equivalent obligations of secrecy where this is necessary and proportionate to reconcile the right of the protection of personal data with the obligation of secrecy.
business_description: This provision allows EU Member States to create specific rules regarding how data protection authorities can interact with organizations bound by professional secrecy, such as lawyers or doctors. It is triggered when a supervisory authority needs to investigate or enforce data protection laws but must also respect the confidentiality obligations of the organization. Organizations must understand that while they have obligations to protect personal data, they also need to maintain confidentiality as required by their professional standards. A common misconception is that data protection laws override all confidentiality obligations, but this provision ensures a balance between the two.

title: Existing data protection rules of churches and religious associations
obligation_text: Where in a Member State, churches and religious associations or communities apply comprehensive rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to processing, such rules may continue to apply, provided that they are brought into line with this Regulation.
business_description: Article 91 of the GDPR allows churches and religious associations in EU Member States to continue using their existing data protection rules, as long as these rules are aligned with the GDPR. This means that if a church or religious community already has comprehensive data protection measures, they can keep using them, but they must ensure these measures meet GDPR standards. This provision is triggered when such organizations are processing personal data and already have established data protection frameworks. It requires them to review and update their rules to ensure compliance with the GDPR, without needing to discard their existing systems entirely.

title: Existing data protection rules of churches and religious associations — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Where in a Member State, churches and religious associations or communities apply comprehensive rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to processing, such rules may continue to apply, provided that they are brought into line with this Regulation.
business_description: This provision applies when churches or religious associations in an EU Member State have their own comprehensive data protection rules. These organizations can continue using their existing rules for processing personal data, but they must ensure these rules align with the GDPR. This means reviewing and updating their policies to meet GDPR standards. It is triggered when such entities are processing personal data under their own frameworks. A common misconception is that these organizations are exempt from GDPR, but they must comply by aligning their rules with the regulation.

title: Exercise of the delegation
obligation_text: The power to adopt delegated acts is conferred on the Commission subject to the conditions laid down in this Article. The power to adopt delegated acts referred to in this Regulation shall be conferred on the Commission for an indeterminate period of time from 24 May 2016.
business_description: Article 92 of the GDPR allows the European Commission to create delegated acts, which are legal acts that supplement or amend non-essential parts of the legislation. This power is granted indefinitely from May 24, 2016. For organizations, this means that the Commission can make certain changes to the GDPR without needing to go through the full legislative process. This is typically triggered when there is a need to update or clarify specific aspects of the regulation. Organizations should stay informed about any new delegated acts as they may impact compliance requirements. A common misconception is that these acts can change core principles of the GDPR, but they only address non-essential elements.

title: Exercise of the delegation — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The power to adopt delegated acts is conferred on the Commission subject to the conditions laid down in this Article. The power to adopt delegated acts referred to in this Regulation shall be conferred on the Commission for an indeterminate period of time from 24 May 2016.
business_description: This article grants the European Commission the authority to create delegated acts related to the GDPR. This means that the Commission can make specific legal changes or clarifications without needing to pass new legislation through the full legislative process. For controllers and processors, this implies that they need to stay informed about any new delegated acts that may affect their compliance obligations. The delegation of this power is ongoing and began on 24 May 2016. A common misconception is that these acts require immediate changes to operations, but they often provide further clarification or detail to existing rules.

title: Committee procedure
obligation_text: The Commission shall be assisted by a committee. That committee shall be a committee within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.
business_description: Article 93 of the GDPR outlines that the European Commission will be supported by a committee when implementing delegated and implementing acts. This means that when the Commission needs to make decisions or adopt measures related to data protection, it consults with a committee as defined by Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. For controllers and processors, this indicates that certain regulatory decisions are made with the input of a specialized committee, ensuring that diverse perspectives are considered. This process is triggered when the Commission needs to adopt specific measures under the GDPR framework. There are no direct actions required from organizations, but understanding this process helps in anticipating regulatory changes.

title: Committee procedure — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The Commission shall be assisted by a committee. That committee shall be a committee within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.
business_description: Article 93.1 of the GDPR outlines that the European Commission will be supported by a committee as per Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. This is relevant when the Commission needs to adopt implementing acts related to data protection. For controllers and processors, this means that certain decisions or regulations that affect data protection practices may be influenced by this committee's input. Organizations should be aware that this committee's role is procedural and does not impose direct obligations on them. A common misconception is that this committee directly regulates data processing activities, but its role is advisory to the Commission.

title: Repeal of Directive 95/46/EC
obligation_text: Directive 95/46/EC is repealed with effect from 25 May 2018. References to the repealed Directive shall be construed as references to this Regulation.
business_description: This article indicates that the older Directive 95/46/EC was officially replaced by the GDPR as of May 25, 2018. For organizations, this means that any references to the old directive in their policies, contracts, or documentation should now be understood as references to the GDPR. This transition requires organizations to ensure that their compliance frameworks are aligned with the GDPR's requirements, rather than those of the repealed directive. A common misconception is that some parts of the old directive might still apply, but in reality, the GDPR fully supersedes it.

title: Repeal of Directive 95/46/EC — paragraph 1
obligation_text: Directive 95/46/EC is repealed with effect from 25 May 2018. References to the repealed Directive shall be construed as references to this Regulation.
business_description: This article indicates that Directive 95/46/EC, which previously governed data protection in the EU, was officially repealed on 25 May 2018. From that date, any references to the old Directive should be understood as referring to the GDPR. For organizations, this means that all data protection practices must align with the GDPR's requirements instead of the old Directive. This transition is crucial for compliance, as it ensures that data processing activities are up to date with the current legal framework. A common misconception is that some parts of the old Directive might still apply, but this is not the case; the GDPR fully replaces it.

title: Relationship with Directive 2002/58/EC
obligation_text: This Regulation shall not impose additional obligations on natural or legal persons in relation to processing in connection with the provision of publicly available electronic communications services in public communications networks in the Union in relation to matters for which they are subject to specific obligations set out in Directive 2002/58/EC.
business_description: Article 95 of the GDPR clarifies that it does not add extra obligations for organizations already governed by Directive 2002/58/EC, which deals with electronic communications services. This means if your organization provides publicly available electronic communications services, you should primarily comply with the specific rules in Directive 2002/58/EC for those services. This article is triggered when your processing activities fall under the scope of both the GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive. A common misconception is that GDPR always adds new requirements, but in this case, it defers to the existing ePrivacy rules.

title: Relationship with Directive 2002/58/EC — paragraph 1
obligation_text: This Regulation shall not impose additional obligations on natural or legal persons in relation to processing in connection with the provision of publicly available electronic communications services in public communications networks in the Union in relation to matters for which they are subject to specific obligations set out in Directive 2002/58/EC.
business_description: Article 95 of the GDPR clarifies that the regulation does not add extra requirements for organizations already covered by Directive 2002/58/EC, which deals with electronic communications services. This means if your organization is providing publicly available electronic communications services, you should primarily follow the specific obligations under Directive 2002/58/EC for data processing related to these services. This article is triggered when processing activities are already regulated by the ePrivacy Directive, ensuring no overlap or conflict between the two sets of rules. A common misconception is that GDPR always adds new obligations, but here it defers to existing rules under the ePrivacy Directive.

title: Relationship with previously concluded Agreements
obligation_text: International agreements involving the transfer of personal data to third countries or international organisations which were concluded by Member States prior to 24 May 2016, and which comply with Union law as applicable prior to that date, shall remain in force until amended, replaced or revoked.
business_description: This provision applies to international agreements made by EU Member States before May 24, 2016, that involve transferring personal data to third countries or international organizations. These agreements can continue to be used as long as they were compliant with EU laws at the time they were made. Organizations should review such agreements to ensure they remain valid and compliant with current data protection standards. This is particularly relevant when considering amendments or replacements to these agreements. A common misconception is that all pre-existing agreements are automatically invalid under GDPR, which is not the case if they meet the specified criteria.

title: Relationship with previously concluded Agreements — paragraph 1
obligation_text: International agreements involving the transfer of personal data to third countries or international organisations which were concluded by Member States prior to 24 May 2016, and which comply with Union law as applicable prior to that date, shall remain in force until amended, replaced or revoked.
business_description: This article addresses international agreements made by EU Member States before May 24, 2016, that involve transferring personal data to countries outside the EU or to international organizations. These agreements can continue to be valid as long as they complied with EU law at the time they were made. Organizations should review such agreements to ensure they still align with current data protection standards and be prepared to update or replace them if necessary. This provision is triggered when dealing with legacy agreements, and it requires organizations to assess their compliance status without assuming they automatically meet current GDPR requirements.

title: Commission reports
obligation_text: By 25 May 2020 and every four years thereafter, the Commission shall submit a report on the evaluation and review of this Regulation to the European Parliament and to the Council. The reports shall be made public.
business_description: Article 97 requires the European Commission to evaluate and review the GDPR and report its findings to the European Parliament and the Council every four years, starting from May 25, 2020. This means that organizations should be aware that the GDPR is subject to periodic review, which may lead to changes or updates in the regulation. However, this article does not impose any direct obligations on controllers or processors. Instead, it ensures that the GDPR remains effective and relevant over time. Organizations should stay informed about these reports as they may influence future compliance requirements.

title: Commission reports — paragraph 1
obligation_text: By 25 May 2020 and every four years thereafter, the Commission shall submit a report on the evaluation and review of this Regulation to the European Parliament and to the Council. The reports shall be made public.
business_description: This article mandates that the European Commission must evaluate and review the GDPR every four years, starting from 25 May 2020. The purpose is to assess how well the regulation is functioning and whether any updates are needed. For organizations, this means staying informed about potential changes to the GDPR that could affect compliance requirements. While the article itself does not impose direct actions on controllers or processors, it highlights the importance of monitoring regulatory updates and being prepared to adapt to any changes that may arise from these evaluations.

title: Review of other Union legal acts on data protection
obligation_text: The Commission shall, if appropriate, submit legislative proposals with a view to amending other Union legal acts on the protection of personal data, in order to ensure uniform and consistent protection of natural persons with regard to processing.
business_description: Article 98 of the GDPR allows the European Commission to propose changes to other EU laws related to data protection. This is to ensure that all laws are aligned and provide consistent protection for individuals' personal data. For controllers and processors, this means staying informed about potential legislative changes that could impact their data protection obligations. This article is triggered when inconsistencies in data protection laws are identified, prompting the Commission to consider amendments. Organizations are not required to take immediate action but should monitor developments to ensure compliance with any new regulations.

title: Review of other Union legal acts on data protection — paragraph 1
obligation_text: The Commission shall, if appropriate, submit legislative proposals with a view to amending other Union legal acts on the protection of personal data, in order to ensure uniform and consistent protection of natural persons with regard to processing.
business_description: Article 98.1 of the GDPR requires the European Commission to review and, if necessary, propose changes to other EU laws related to data protection. This ensures that all laws align and provide consistent protection for individuals' personal data. For controllers and processors, this means staying informed about potential legislative changes that could affect their data protection obligations. It is triggered when inconsistencies are identified between the GDPR and other EU legal acts. Organizations should monitor updates from the Commission to ensure compliance with any new or amended laws. A common misconception is that this article imposes direct obligations on organizations, but it primarily guides legislative actions at the EU level.

title: Entry into force and application
obligation_text: This Regulation shall enter into force on the twentieth day following that of its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union. It shall apply from 25 May 2018.
business_description: Article 99 of the GDPR specifies when the regulation became legally effective. It was published in the Official Journal of the European Union and came into force 20 days later. However, organizations were required to comply with its provisions starting from 25 May 2018. This means that from this date, all data controllers and processors had to ensure their operations were in line with GDPR requirements. A common misconception is that the regulation applied immediately upon publication, but there was a transition period allowing organizations to prepare for compliance.

title: Entry into force and application — paragraph 1
obligation_text: This Regulation shall enter into force on the twentieth day following that of its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union.
business_description: This article specifies when the GDPR becomes legally binding. It is triggered when the GDPR is published in the Official Journal of the European Union. Organizations, including data controllers and processors, need to be aware that the GDPR takes effect 20 days after this publication date. This means they must ensure compliance with its provisions by this time. A common misconception is that the GDPR applies immediately upon publication, but there is a 20-day period before it becomes enforceable. Organizations should use this time to finalize their compliance measures.

title: Entry into force and application — paragraph 2
obligation_text: It shall apply from 25 May 2018.
business_description: This article specifies the date when the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) became applicable, which is May 25, 2018. For controllers and processors, this means that from this date, they must comply with all the requirements set out in the GDPR. It is important for organizations to understand that any data processing activities conducted after this date must adhere to GDPR standards. A common misconception is that GDPR compliance was optional before this date, but in reality, it became mandatory for all relevant entities starting on May 25, 2018.
